1: shamor et-chodesh ha'aviv ve'asita Pesach l'YHWH Eloheicha ki be'chodesh ha'aviv hotziacha YHWH Eloheicha miMitzrayim laylah (šāmôr ʾet-ḥōdeš hāʾābîb wĕʿāśîtā peseḥ laYhwh ʾĕlōhêkā kî bĕḥōdeš hāʾābîb hôṣîʾăkā Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā mimmmiṣrayim lāylāh): 'Observe the month of Abib and keep the Passover to the LORD your God, for in the month of Abib the LORD your God brought you out of Egypt by night.' Chodesh ha'aviv (month of the green ears/spring grain) — later called Nisan in the post-exilic calendar. Pesach (Passover) from pasach (to pass over, to skip; also possibly to protect or leap over). The Passover is the founding festival of Israel's identity as a redeemed people: the night of exodus (laylah) is the pivot of the nation's existence. Paul explicitly identifies Christ as the Passover lamb (1 Cor 5:7: our Passover lamb, Christ, has been sacrificed), and John times the crucifixion to coincide with the slaughter of the Passover lambs (John 19:14,31).
20: tzedek tzedek tirdof lema'an tichyeh veyarashta et-ha'aretz asher YHWH Eloheicha noten lach (ṣedeq ṣedeq tirdōp̄ lĕmaʿan tiḥyeh wĕyāraštā ʾet-hāʾāreṣ ʾăšer Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā nōtēn lāk): 'Justice, and only justice, you shall pursue, that you may live and inherit the land that the LORD your God is giving you.' Tzedek tzedek tirdof — the doubled noun is for emphasis (justice, justice!) rather than mere redundancy: the repetition insists on justice that is thoroughgoing, not merely formal. Tirdof (you shall pursue/chase) is hunting vocabulary — active, vigorous pursuit of justice as one pursues prey, not passive compliance when convenient. The connection between justice and land-possession (lema'an tikhyeh ve-yarashta) makes the covenant's social ethics directly tied to covenant standing in the land. Amos 5:24 and Micah 6:8 echo the same conviction that covenant fidelity is measured in justice done to the vulnerable.
2: vezavaḥta pesaḥ laYHWH Eloheicha tzon uvakar bmakom asher yivḥar YHWH: 'You shall sacrifice the Passover offering to YHWH your God — from the flock or the herd — at the place YHWH will choose.' Deuteronomy's Passover is centralized: unlike the household Passover of Exod 12 (slaughtered by families in their homes), Deuteronomy's version requires the central sanctuary. ṣōʾn ûḇāqār (flock or herd) — Deuteronomy expands the Passover offering beyond Exodus's exclusive lamb requirement to include cattle, accommodating larger congregational celebration at the central shrine. The centralization reflects the covenant's post-conquest form: the scattered household practice of Exodus becomes the national communal feast of Deuteronomy.
3: lo-tokhal alav ḥametz shivat yamim tokhal alav matzot leḥem oni: 'You shall eat no leavened bread with it. For seven days you shall eat unleavened bread — the bread of affliction (leḥem ʿōnî).' leḥem ʿōnî (bread of affliction/poverty/suffering) — the unleavened bread is designated as memory-food, recalling Egyptian slavery. ʿōnî connects to ʿānāh (to afflict, humble), the verb used of Egypt's oppression of Israel (Exod 1:11,12; Deut 26:7). Eating the flat, poor-man's bread annually reconnects every generation to the pre-Exodus experience of affliction. The NT's Last Supper uses this unleavened bread of Passover as the vehicle for Jesus's self-identification: 'This is my body' (Matt 26:26), giving the bread of affliction a new meaning — the broken body that absorbs the affliction.
4: velo yera'eh lekha seor bekol-gevulekha shivat yamim velo yalin min-habasar: 'No leaven shall be seen anywhere in your territory for seven days; and none of the flesh you sacrifice on the evening of the first day shall remain overnight until morning.' The thoroughness of leaven-removal (wᵉlōʾ yērāʾeh lᵉkā śᵉʾōr bᵉkol-gᵉḇûlᵉkā šiḇʿat yāmîm) signals that the festival's memorial function requires a complete change in the household's material environment. Paul's metaphor of the unleavened community (1 Cor 5:6-8) draws directly on this comprehensive leaven-removal: 'Cleanse out the old leaven... let us keep the feast, not with the old leaven of malice and evil, but with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth.'
5: lo tukhal lizboaḥ et-hapesaḥ be'aḥad she'areykha: 'You may not sacrifice the Passover within any of your towns that YHWH your God is giving you.' The repeated prohibition of local sacrifice (following the centralization principle of ch. 12) applies explicitly to the Passover. In Exodus, the Passover was definitionally a household affair; Deuteronomy's transformation of it into a pilgrimage festival (ḥag) is one of the most significant legal-liturgical developments in the Pentateuch. The centralization is not a restriction of piety but an expansion: the household meal becomes the national gathering, the local memorial becomes the pan-Israel covenant renewal.
6: ki im el-hamakom asher yivḥar YHWH Eloheicha leshaken shemo sham tizbah et-hapesaḥ: 'Rather, at the place YHWH your God will choose to make his name dwell, there you shall sacrifice the Passover.' lᵉšakkēn šᵉmô šām (to make his name dwell there) — the name-theology again: YHWH's presence is mediated through the name, not through physical residence. ba'erev keḇo' hashemesh (in the evening at sunset) — the timing preserves the original Passover chronology (Exod 12:6: ‘between the evenings’). The evening timing creates a nocturnal feast-memorial, connecting the darkness of the original Egyptian night when the destroyer passed over with the annual darkness of communal memorial.
7: uḇishalta ve'akhaleta bamakom asher yivḥar YHWH Eloheicha bo: 'You shall cook and eat it at the place YHWH your God will choose, and in the morning you shall return to your tents.' ubashalta veakhaleta (you shall cook and eat) — contrast with Exod 12:8-9's prohibition against boiling: the Exodus Passover was roasted; Deuteronomy permits boiling. This apparent contradiction was resolved in 2 Chr 35:13 ('they roasted the Passover lambs with fire as prescribed, and they boiled the holy offerings in pots') by distinguishing the lamb (roasted) from accompanying offerings (boiled). ûḇabbōqer wᵉpānîtā wᵉhālaḵtā lᵉʾohāleḵā (in the morning you shall return to your tents) — the overnight-to-morning feast structure mirrors the original Passover night.
8: sheshet yamim tokhal matzot uvayyom hashevii atzeret laYHWH Eloheicha: 'For six days you shall eat unleavened bread, and on the seventh day there shall be a solemn assembly (ʿăṣeret) for YHWH your God.' ʿăṣeret (solemn assembly, assembly of restraint) — from ʿāṣar (to restrain, hold back): a day of stopping, of gathering for the purpose of assembly. The same term designates the eighth day of Sukkot (Lev 23:36; Num 29:35) and the closing assembly of Shavuot. The seventh-day assembly gives the seven-day Passover/Unleavened Bread festival a Sabbath-like closure: it ends with a day of cessation and gathering before YHWH, structuring the festival in the Sabbath pattern.
9: shivah shavuot tispor-lakh: 'Count off seven weeks.' The counting of seven weeks (seven times seven days = forty-nine days) from the beginning of harvest inaugurates the second major festival, Shavuot (Feast of Weeks, Pentecost). The agricultural trigger — meḥel ḥermesh baqqamah (when the sickle is first put to the standing grain) — roots the festival in the harvest cycle: Shavuot celebrates the completion of the grain harvest that began at Passover. The counting itself (tispor-lakh, count for yourself) is a personal obligation, not just a calendar observation; the individual's act of counting participates in the communal memorial.
10: ve'asita ḥag shavuot laYHWH Eloheicha middat nedavat yadkha: 'Then you shall celebrate the Feast of Weeks to YHWH your God with a freewill offering (nedāḇāh) from your hand.' mîddat nidaḇat yāḏᵉkā (in proportion to the freewill offering of your hand) — the Shavuot contribution is calibrated to the giver's capacity, not fixed at a set amount. The nedāḇāh (freewill offering) is the opposite of a compelled tax; it is the worshipper's voluntary response to YHWH's blessing. kaʾăšer yᵉḇārᵉkekā YHWH Eloheycha (as YHWH your God has blessed you) — the amount is determined by what YHWH has given, not by external obligation. The NT applies this proportionality principle to financial giving (2 Cor 9:7: 'each one must give as he has decided in his heart, not reluctantly or under compulsion').
11: vesamaḥta lifnei YHWH Eloheicha atah uvinekha uvittekha ve'avdekha va'amatkha vehallevi: 'You shall rejoice before YHWH your God — you, your son and daughter, your male and female servants, the Levite, the resident alien, the fatherless, and the widow.' The inclusion list for the Shavuot rejoicing is Deuteronomy's most comprehensive social gathering: the household (you, sons, daughters), the servants (male and female), the landless religious (Levite), and the three categories of the vulnerable (resident alien, fatherless, widow). vesamaḥta (you shall rejoice) is a command, not merely permission: joy before YHWH is a covenant obligation, not an optional emotional response. The festival's shared rejoicing enacts the covenant community's equality before YHWH.
12: vezakarta ki-eved hayita beMitzrayim veshomarta vaasita et-hakhukkim ha'eleh: 'Remember that you were a slave in Egypt, and be careful to observe these statutes.' The Exodus-memory motivation reappears for Shavuot. The memory of Egyptian slavery is the motivational foundation for the inclusive festival: because Israel was once enslaved and excluded, its festivals must include every vulnerable person. The covenant community's ethic of inclusion is grounded in the narrative of redemption from exclusion. Later Judaism associated Shavuot with the giving of Torah at Sinai, and the NT's Pentecost (Acts 2) — on the day of Shavuot — frames the Spirit-giving as the new Torah-writing (Jer 31:33; 2 Cor 3:3).
13: ḥag hassukkot ta'aseh lekha shivat yamim be'aspekha migornekha umiyiqvekha: 'You shall celebrate the Feast of Booths (ḥag hassûkkôt) for seven days, when you have gathered in the produce from your threshing floor and your winepress.' Sukkot is the third and most joyful pilgrimage festival, celebrating the autumn harvest's completion. mi-gārnᵉkā ûmi-yiqᵉḇkā (from your threshing floor and your winepress) — the final grain and wine harvest is gathered before the festival; Sukkot is the culmination of the agricultural year. The festival's booth-dwelling (sukkāh) recalls Israel's wilderness journey (Lev 23:42-43): the temporary shelters ground the harvest celebration in the memory of dependence on YHWH during the desert years.
14: vesamaḥta beḥaggekha atah uvinekha uvittekha ve'avdekha va'amatkha vehallevi veha-ger vehayatom veha'almana: 'You shall rejoice in your feast — you, your son and daughter, your male and female servants, the Levite, the resident alien, the fatherless, and the widow.' The sevenfold inclusion-list (the same as v. 11 for Shavuot) is repeated for Sukkot, emphasizing that the harvest celebration's fullness is measured by its comprehensiveness. vesamaḥta (you shall rejoice) — joy as covenant command: the harvest celebration that excludes any of these groups is incomplete, whatever its external magnificence. Jesus's parable of the great banquet (Luke 14:21-23) echoes this inclusive-feast logic.
15: shivat yamim taḥog laYHWH Eloheicha bamakom asher yivḥar YHWH: 'For seven days you shall keep the feast to YHWH your God at the place he will choose, for YHWH your God will bless you in all your produce and in all the work of your hands, so that you will be altogether joyful.' wᵉhāyîtā ʾak śāmēaḥ (you shall be altogether joyful, only joyful) — the adverb ʾak (only, surely) intensifies: the seven days of Sukkot are to be marked by unqualified joy. This is the basis for Nehemiah's pronouncement when the Torah was read on Rosh Hashanah: 'the joy of the LORD is your strength' (Neh 8:10) — the covenant community's festival joy is not circumstantial but commanded and therefore sustainable.
16: shalosh paamim bashanah yera'eh khal-zakhurkha et-pnei YHWH Eloheicha bamakom asher yivḥar: 'Three times a year all your males shall appear before YHWH your God at the place he will choose: at the Feast of Unleavened Bread, at the Feast of Weeks, and at the Feast of Booths.' The three-festival pilgrimage system (šālôš pᵉʿāmîm baššānāh) structures the agricultural-religious calendar into a triple annual encounter with YHWH. yēraʾeh kol-zᵉkûrᵉkā ʾet-pᵉnê YHWH (all your males shall appear before YHWH's face) — the idiom 'appear before the face' is the audience-before-the-king formula: the pilgrimage feast is an annual royal audience. velo yera'eh et-pnei YHWH reiqam (no one shall appear before YHWH empty-handed) — presence requires offering; the audience is never empty-handed.
17: ish kemattnat yado kevirkhat YHWH Eloheicha asher natan-lakh: 'Every man shall give as he is able, in proportion to the blessing YHWH your God has given him.' The principle of proportional giving — kᵉmattnat yāḏô (according to the gift of his hand, i.e., his capacity) — is the foundation of Israel's festival economics. The measure is not equal contribution but equal sacrifice proportional to means: the rich man's obligation is greater because his capacity is greater. This principle governs the NT's theology of giving (2 Cor 8:12-13: 'if the readiness is there, it is acceptable according to what a person has, not according to what he does not have') and the assessment of the widow's mite (Mark 12:43-44: 'out of her poverty she put in everything she had').
18: shofetim veshotrim titten lekha bekhol-she'areykha asher YHWH Eloheicha noten lekha lishvatekha: 'You shall appoint judges (šōpᵉṭîm) and officials (šōṭᵉrîm) throughout all your towns.' The administrative section (16:18-17:13) establishes the judicial infrastructure alongside the festival calendar, making the transition from cultic to civil law natural within the covenant framework. šōpᵉṭîm (judges) exercise decision-making authority; šōṭᵉrîm (officials) are enforcement officers or administrators. The town-by-town appointment ensures local access to justice: unlike Mesopotamian systems where courts were concentrated at major urban centers, Israel's covenant calls for justice at every town gate (bᵉkol-šᵉʿāreykā).
19: lo-tatteh mishpat lo-takir panim velo-tikkaḥ shohad: 'Do not pervert justice (mishpāṭ); do not show favoritism (pānîm); do not accept a bribe (šōḥaḏ).' The three judicial prohibitions target the three primary corruption mechanisms: substantive injustice (warping the verdict), procedural partiality (recognizing faces = social bias), and financial corruption (bribery). ki haššōḥaḏ yᵉʿawwēr ʿênê ḥăkāmîm wîsallēp diḇrê ṣaddîqîm (for a bribe blinds the eyes of the wise and subverts the words of the righteous) — the two effects of bribery: cognitive blindness in the judge and rhetorical corruption of the verdict-speech. The prophetic tradition returns repeatedly to these three corruptions (Amos 5:12; Isa 1:23; Mic 3:11; Ezek 22:12).
21: lo-tita lekha ashera kol-etz etzel mizbah YHWH Eloheicha: 'You shall not set up any wooden Asherah pole beside the altar of YHWH your God that you build.' The prohibition against the ʾăšērāh (wooden pole representing the Canaanite goddess Asherah) placed alongside YHWH's altar reflects the syncretistic tendency that repeatedly compromised Israelite worship. Placing an Asherah beside YHWH's altar was not outright rejection of YHWH but polytheistic accommodation — the very pattern the Deuteronomic reform under Josiah (2 Kgs 23:4-7,14-15) systematically eliminated. The prohibition is not about all trees but about trees erected as cult objects beside YHWH's altar.
22: velo-taqim lekha mattzeva asher sane YHWH Eloheicha: 'And do not erect a sacred stone pillar (maṣṣēḇāh), for YHWH your God hates these.' The maṣṣēḇāh (standing stone, sacred pillar) is contextually prohibited: in earlier patriarchal narratives, Jacob erects maṣṣēḇôt without censure (Gen 28:18; 31:13,45; 35:14,20). Deuteronomy's prohibition targets not standing stones per se but their use in Canaanite religious practice: by the time of Deuteronomy's address, standing stones had been fully co-opted as Canaanite religious objects, making their use by Israel indistinguishable from Canaanite worship. Context determines whether an object is licit or illicit; the Canaanite cultural context has made the maṣṣēḇāh an idolatry-marker.
